data.content:
### Create Slash Command with Arguments Placeholder

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/common-workflows

This command creates a Markdown file for a slash command that accepts user input. The '$ARGUMENTS' placeholder in the file content will be replaced by any text provided after the command invocation, allowing for dynamic prompts.

```bash
echo 'Find and fix issue #$ARGUMENTS. Follow these steps: 1. Understand the issue described in the ticket 2. Locate the relevant code in our codebase 3. Implement a solution that addresses the root cause 4. Add appropriate tests 5. Prepare a concise PR description' > .claude/commands/fix-issue.md
```

--------------------------------

### Example Slash Command for Code Review

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/slash-commands

This markdown file defines a simple slash command for code review. It outlines specific areas to check, such as security vulnerabilities, performance issues, and code style violations. This command is intended for manual invocation.

```markdown
# .claude/commands/review.md
Review this code for:
- Security vulnerabilities
- Performance issues
- Code style violations
```

--------------------------------

### MCP Slash Command - Without Arguments

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/slash-commands

This snippet demonstrates how to call an MCP slash command that does not require any arguments. These commands are dynamically discovered from connected MCP servers.

```shell
/mcp__github__list_prs
```

--------------------------------

### Validating YAML Frontmatter (Bash)

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills

Provides Bash commands to inspect the frontmatter of a SKILL.md file and check for common YAML syntax errors. This helps troubleshoot issues where Claude might not load a Skill due to invalid formatting.

```bash
# View frontmatter
cat .claude/skills/my-skill/SKILL.md | head -n 15

# Check for common issues
# - Missing opening or closing ---
# - Tabs instead of spaces
# - Unquoted strings with special characters
```

--------------------------------

### Define Personal Slash Command Content

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/common-workflows

This command creates a Markdown file (e.g., 'security-review.md') within the '~/.claude/commands' directory. The content of this file defines the prompt for a personal slash command that can be used consistently across different projects.

```bash
echo "Review this code for security vulnerabilities, focusing on:" > ~/.claude/commands/security-review.md
```

--------------------------------

### MCP Slash Command - With Arguments

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/slash-commands

This snippet illustrates calling MCP slash commands that accept arguments. The arguments follow the command name and are processed by the MCP server.

```shell
/mcp__github__pr_review 456
/mcp__jira__create_issue "Bug title" high
```

--------------------------------

### Enabling SlashCommand Tool Usage

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/slash-commands

This snippet shows how to encourage Claude to use the SlashCommand tool by referencing a custom slash command in a prompt. This makes the command available for programmatic execution.

```markdown
> Run /write-unit-test when you are about to start writing tests.
```

--------------------------------

### Ask Claude Code About Slash Commands

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/quickstart

Requests guidance from Claude Code on how to use its available slash commands.

```text
> how do I use slash commands in Claude Code?
```

--------------------------------

### Define Project-Specific Slash Command Content

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/common-workflows

This command creates a Markdown file (e.g., 'optimize.md') within the '.claude/commands' directory. The content of this file serves as the prompt that Claude Code will execute when the corresponding slash command (e.g., '/optimize') is invoked.

```bash
echo "Analyze the performance of this code and suggest three specific optimizations:" > .claude/commands/optimize.md
```

--------------------------------

### Disabling SlashCommand Tool Globally

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/slash-commands

This bash snippet demonstrates how to disable the entire SlashCommand tool, preventing Claude from executing any slash commands. This is done by adding 'SlashCommand' to the deny rules in the permissions configuration.

```bash
/permissions
# Add to deny rules: SlashCommand
```

--------------------------------

### Create Personal Slash Command Directory

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/common-workflows

This command creates a directory named '~/.claude/commands' in the user's home directory. This location is used for storing personal slash commands that are accessible across all projects on the user's machine.

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.claude/commands
```

--------------------------------

### GitHub Workflow Using Claude Code Action with Slash Commands

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/github-actions

This workflow showcases the use of Claude Code Action with slash commands for automated code reviews. Triggered on pull request events, it uses the `/review` command to initiate a review process. The `claude_args` parameter is used to specify the maximum number of turns for the conversation, ensuring focused reviews.

```yaml
name: Code Review
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]
jobs:
  review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          anthropic_api_key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          prompt: "/review"
          claude_args: "--max-turns 5"
```

--------------------------------

### SlashCommand Permission Rules - Prefix Match

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/slash-commands

This snippet demonstrates a prefix match permission rule for the SlashCommand tool, allowing a command and any of its arguments to be invoked. The asterisk wildcard enables flexibility in command usage.

```text
SlashCommand:/review-pr:*
```

--------------------------------

### Execute MCP Prompt as Slash Command (No Arguments)

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp

Example of invoking an MCP server-defined prompt using Claude's slash command interface. The format is '/mcp__servername__promptname'.

```text
> /mcp__github__list_prs
```

--------------------------------

### SlashCommand Permission Rules - Exact Match

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/slash-commands

This snippet illustrates an exact match permission rule for the SlashCommand tool, allowing only a specific command with no arguments to be invoked. This provides fine-grained control over command execution.

```text
SlashCommand:/commit
```

--------------------------------

### Execute MCP Prompt as Slash Command (With Arguments)

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp

Demonstrates invoking MCP prompts that require arguments, passed space-separated after the command. Examples include GitHub pull request review and Jira issue creation.

```text
> /mcp__github__pr_review 456

> /mcp__jira__create_issue "Bug in login flow" high
```

--------------------------------

### Create Project-Specific Slash Command Directory

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/common-workflows

This command creates a directory named '.claude/commands' in the current project for storing custom command files. This directory structure is used by Claude Code to identify and manage project-specific commands.

```bash
mkdir -p .claude/commands
```

--------------------------------

### Execute Bash Commands Directly

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/interactive-mode

Run bash commands directly in Claude Code by prefixing the command with '!'. This mode adds the command and its output to the conversation context, shows real-time progress, and supports backgrounding long-running commands. It does not require Claude's interpretation or approval.

```bash
! npm test
! git status
! ls -la
```

--------------------------------

### Plugin-Prefixed Command Invocation

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/slash-commands

This snippet illustrates how to invoke a command when disambiguation is needed, typically by prefixing the command with its plugin name. This ensures the correct command is executed.

```shell
/plugin-name:command-name
```

--------------------------------

### Define Path-Specific Rules with YAML Frontmatter

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory

This example demonstrates how to scope rules to specific files using YAML frontmatter with the 'paths' field. These conditional rules only apply when Claude is working with files matching the specified glob patterns. The 'theme={null}' attribute is typically used for rendering purposes and does not affect the rule logic.

```markdown
---
paths: src/api/**/*.ts
---

# API Development Rules

- All API endpoints must include input validation
- Use the standard error response format
- Include OpenAPI documentation comments
```

--------------------------------

### Sandboxing Commands with Sandbox Runtime

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing

Use the open-source sandbox runtime npm package to sandbox other programs or commands. This bash command demonstrates how to execute a specified command within the sandbox environment using the `npx @anthropic-ai/sandbox-runtime` command.

```bash
npx @anthropic-ai/sandbox-runtime <command-to-sandbox>
```

--------------------------------

### Command Invocation with Arguments

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/slash-commands

This snippet shows how to invoke a command that accepts arguments. Placeholders like `arg1` and `arg2` represent the values that can be passed to the command.

```shell
/command-name arg1 arg2
```

--------------------------------

### Replace System Prompt with Bash Command

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/cli-reference

This example demonstrates replacing the entire default system prompt with custom instructions using the `--system-prompt` flag in a bash command. This gives complete control over Claude's behavior.

```bash
claude --system-prompt "You are a Python expert who only writes type-annotated code"
```

--------------------------------

### Append to System Prompt with Bash Command

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/cli-reference

This example illustrates appending custom instructions to the default system prompt using the `--append-system-prompt` flag in a bash command. This is recommended for most use cases as it preserves Claude Code's default capabilities.

```bash
claude --append-system-prompt "Always use TypeScript and include JSDoc comments"
```

--------------------------------

### Multi-file Skill Configuration (YAML)

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills

YAML frontmatter for a multi-file Claude Skill, specifying its name, description, and required packages (pypdf, pdfplumber). This Skill handles PDF processing tasks and resides in a directory with multiple components.

```yaml
---
name: pdf-processing
description: Extract text, fill forms, merge PDFs. Use when working with PDF files, forms, or document extraction. Requires pypdf and pdfplumber packages.
---

# PDF Processing

```

--------------------------------

### Configuring Skill with Tool Permissions (YAML)

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills

Example YAML frontmatter for a Claude Skill that requires specific tool permissions (Read, Grep, Glob). It outlines the Skill's purpose, allowed tools, and a review checklist.

```yaml
---
name: code-reviewer
description: Review code for best practices and potential issues. Use when reviewing code, checking PRs, or analyzing code quality.
allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob
---

# Code Reviewer

## Review checklist

1. Code organization and structure
2. Error handling
3. Performance considerations
4. Security concerns
5. Test coverage

## Instructions

1. Read the target files using Read tool
2. Search for patterns using Grep
3. Find related files using Glob
4. Provide detailed feedback on code quality
```

--------------------------------

### Create Custom Output Style - Markdown Example

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/output-styles

This snippet demonstrates the structure of a custom output style file using Markdown. It includes frontmatter for metadata such as the style's name and description, followed by the custom instructions for the assistant's behavior.

```markdown
```markdown  theme={null}
---
name: My Custom Style
description:
  A brief description of what this style does, to be displayed to the user
---

# Custom Style Instructions

You are an interactive CLI tool that helps users with software engineering
tasks. [Your custom instructions here...]

## Specific Behaviors

[Define how the assistant should behave in this style...]
```
```

--------------------------------

### Log Bash Commands with jq

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide

This hook logs shell commands executed by Claude Code, specifically filtering for Bash commands. It uses `jq` to parse the tool input and append the command and its description to a log file. This is useful for auditing and debugging command execution.

```bash
jq -r '"\(.tool_input.command) - \(.tool_input.description // "No description")"' >> ~/.claude/bash-command-log.txt
```

--------------------------------

### Direct Command Invocation

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/slash-commands

This snippet demonstrates the basic invocation of a command in Claude Code when there are no naming conflicts. It shows the simplest form of calling a command.

```shell
/command-name
```

--------------------------------

### Configuring Simple Skill (YAML)

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills

Example YAML frontmatter for a simple Claude Skill contained within a single SKILL.md file. It defines the Skill's name, a clear description, and instructions for its usage.

```yaml
---
name: generating-commit-messages
description: Generates clear commit messages from git diffs. Use when writing commit messages or reviewing staged changes.
---

# Generating Commit Messages

## Instructions

1. Run `git diff --staged` to see changes
2. I'll suggest a commit message with:
   - Summary under 50 characters
   - Detailed description
   - Affected components

## Best practices

- Use present tense
- Explain what and why, not how
```

--------------------------------

### Verify Bash Command Log

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide

This command is used to view the contents of the bash command log file created by the hook. It allows users to verify that commands are being logged as expected after Claude Code executes them.

```bash
cat ~/.claude/bash-command-log.txt
```

--------------------------------

### Example: Mixed Name and Command Allowlist for MCP Servers (JSON)

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp

This JSON configuration shows a mixed allowlist for MCP servers, including both server names and commands. Stdio servers must match a command entry if command restrictions exist. Remote servers can match by name only. The denylist takes precedence over the allowlist.

```json
{
  "allowedMcpServers": [
    { "serverName": "github" },
    { "serverCommand": ["npx", "-y", "approved-package"] }
  ]
}
```

--------------------------------

### Example Bash Commands Not Tracked by Checkpointing

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/checkpointing

Checkpointing in Claude Code does not track file modifications made by bash commands. The following examples illustrate bash commands whose file changes will not be undoable via the rewind feature.

```bash
rm file.txt
mv old.txt new.txt
cp source.txt dest.txt
```

--------------------------------

### File Path Matching for Read & Edit Permissions

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/iam

Illustrates how Read and Edit permissions are applied to files using patterns similar to gitignore. It covers absolute paths, home directory paths, paths relative to the settings file, and paths relative to the current directory.

```plaintext
//path
~/path
/path
path or ./path

Read(//Users/alice/secrets/**)
Read(~/Documents/*.pdf)
Edit(/src/**/*.ts)
Read(*.env)

Edit(/docs/**)
Read(~/.zshrc)
Edit(//tmp/scratch.txt)
Read(src/**)
```
